paper_title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-to-hidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report a twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents Dhaval C. Patel*

sections.title: Tanisha Rathod Aaron Fan
sections.content: Tianjun Feng Chun-Yi Tsai Yihan Sun Sanskruti Vijay Shejwal Sam Colman

sections.title: Amaan Sheikh Dev Bahl Ann Li Krish Veera Alimurtaza Mustafa Merchant
sections.content: Shambhawi Baswaraj Bhure Thomas Ajai Rujing Li Vivek G. Iyer Darief Maes Yassine Jebbouri Yuval Shemla Yeshitha Bhuvanesh Rushin Bhatt

sections.title: Shriya Aishani Rachakonda Yunfeng Chen Aryaman Agrawal Aman Upganlawar
sections.content: Mao Le Jonathan Ang Yubin Sally Go Madhav Rajkondawar Yang-Jung Chen

sections.title: Shen Li Charles Xu Byeolah Kwon
Introduction
sections.content: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrialagent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregatescore leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-tohidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with pre-registered pilot design and field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report. *Corresponding author: pateldha@us.ibm.com 1 The evaluation of LLM agents has outgrown its leaderboards. Agents today plan, call tools, reuse artifacts across turns, and coordinate with other agents, yet they are ranked by small number of aggregate scores inherited from single-shot benchmarks. The result is rank instability. In recent 149-team agentic competition (Patel et al., 2026), the Spearman correlation between publicleaderboard rankings and hidden-evaluation rankings was ρ = 0.13 on the execution track (n = 13, statistically indistinguishable from zero) and ρ = 0.69 on the planning track (n = 20), with the planning tracks upper 95% confidence bound falling at the falsification threshold we propose. Aggregate scores do not predict what an operator would observe in deployment. Two streams of work have responded to this gap. The first is wave of agent benchmarks (Jimenez et al., 2024; Yao et al., 2024; Froger et al., 2025; Luo et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2026; Patel et al., 2025; Bandel et al., 2026); each surfaces different facet of trajectory-level evaluation, but each still ranks configurations by aggregate score. The second is longer-running NLP critique of singlescore ranking (Ethayarajh and Jurafsky, 2020; Bowman and Dahl, 2021; Raji et al., 2021), multidimensional frameworks (Liang et al., 2022; Kiela et al., 2021), and behavioral testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020). HELM broadens the dimensions singleshot model is measured on, but agents introduce orthogonal axes (orchestration, multi-turn artifact reuse, tool-call hygiene, judge-independent veriG7 Battery (NASA Li-ion) G8 Transformer (IEC std.) G30 PHMForge (8 classes) G23 Visual Inspect (4 cls., MM) G8 Auto Scenario-Gen G12 Protocol Orchestration G30 MCP-vs-RAG ablation G21 Reasoning on/off G20 QLoRA tool internalize G14 Adaptive routing

sections.title: Orchestration
sections.content: AssetOpsBench 141 scenarios HVAC chillers Plan-Execute

sections.title: Reasoning Mode
sections.content: Knowledge / Retrieval

sections.title: Infrastructure
sections.content: G5 Supervisor-Specialist G12 Verified PE + Self-Ask G30 ReAct / Claude Code G3 Knowledge Plugin MCP G14 Confidence-gated routing G9 Temporal semantic cache G16 Hedged FMSR dispatch G19 TTM Chronos swap G27 torch.compile + GPU G7 MCP-batching cache Figure 1: Fourteen extensions of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark along six axes. Group identifiers index Section 5. fication) that HELM does not score; recent calls to treat interactive evaluation as design science (Xuan et al., 2026) name the structural change without proposing ranking criterion. The contribution of this paper is that ranking criterion: predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, paired with twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the dimensions current leaderboards collapse. We study the problem in industrial asset operations and maintenance, where the stakes of mis-evaluation are concrete (operator time, capital, safety) and where fourteen recent implementation studies extending one MCP-based benchmark have surfaced complementary failure modes the original benchmarks headline metric does not see. We argue for particular position: Position. Aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify the dimensions on which deployed LLM agents are evaluated. The field would benefit from multi-tier measurement apparatus ranked by predictive validity rather than in-sample mean. The position rests on three supporting claims, each developed below: 1. Existing benchmarks measure overlapping subsets of larger measurement space; their findings, taken together, suggest the larger space is non-redundant. 2. Within single industrial domain, parallel implementations by independent teams converge on evaluation dimensions that no single benchmark surfaces. 3. Predictive validity; how in-sample rankings forecast out-of-sample rankings; is more useful ranking criterion than in-sample mean for deployment decisions. This paper draws on three kinds of evidence. First, seven recent benchmark and deployedsystem papers (AssetOpsBench, MCP-Bench, MCP-Universe, ARE/Gaia2, TaskBench (Shen et al., 2024), Exgentic, and the Condition Insight industrial CMMS framework (ODonncha et al., 2026)) whose distinctive metrics we consolidate. Second, fourteen parallel implementation studies (See Figure 1) of MCP-based agentic systems in industrial asset operations and maintenance, each producing complementary failure-mode observations (Li et al., 2026b,c,a; Mazeeva et al., 2026; Merchant et al., 2026; Gowda et al., 2026; Kumar et al., 2026; Agarwal et al., 2026; Feng et al., 2026; Bhandari et al., 2026; Jebbouri et al., 2026; Go et al., 2026; Vinod et al., 2026). Third, cross-paper triangulation: findings observed independently by multiple teams under different architectures provide stronger inference than any single empirical study can. Contributions. (1) synthesis of evaluation dimensions across seven source benchmarks, organized into twelve-tier measurement apparatus; 2 (2) position argument that aggregate-score leaderboards underspecify deployment-relevant evaluation; (3) three concrete predictive-validity criteria (held-out, cross-subset, adversarial) for testing whether the position holds; (4) predictive-validity leaderboard schema with implementation guidance; (5) falsifiable research agenda for empirical validation, with explicit acknowledgment of what we have not yet tested. Benchmark choice. We anchor the synthesis on AssetOpsBench (Patel et al., 2025) because, at time of writing, it satisfies four criteria that distinguish serious deployment-oriented agent benchmark on the public information available: (i) community traction (more than 1,600 GitHub stars and 230 forks); (ii) cross-venue uptake (accepted publications at EMNLP 2025, NeurIPS 2025, and AAAI 2026); (iii) demonstrated extensibility (used as the substrate of the 149-team CODS-2025 competition (Patel et al., 2026) and of the fourteen implementation studies aggregated in this paper, each adding scenarios, asset classes, or architectural variants); and (iv) full public availability of the benchmark scenarios, MCP server tooling, and Docker artifacts, which is what made deep-dive replication tractable for those teams. Scope. This is position paper grounded in synthesis; we do not run new controlled experiments. The fourteen implementation studies were selected for currency, depth of extension along single axis (each study modifies one architectural variable end-to-end), and coverage of complementary tiers of the apparatus we propose. We therefore frame the evidence as convergent architectural sensitivity rather than independent triangulation: the same benchmark surfaces different failure modes under different architectural choices. We commit to the falsification conditions in Section 4.3.

sections.title: 2 The Argument
sections.content: The position rests on three structural critiques of aggregate-score leaderboards; the transferability assumption, judge reflexivity, and the circularity through which evaluation constitutes the capability it measures (Kalaitzidis, 2026); developed below.

sections.title: Dimensions
sections.content: The core argument: Pass1 score of 0.75 can be achieved by many qualitatively different configurations; one that is reasoning-heavy and costexpensive, one that is retrieval-rich and latencybound, one that is tool-hygiene-fragile but artifactreuse-efficient. Aggregate scores treat these as equivalent; deployment treats them as not. Three concrete cases of aggregation hiding qualitativelydistinct behavior, each later re-introduced as sensitivity case in Section 5: Per-rubric reasoning sensitivity. Reasoningon vs. reasoning-off configurations score similarly on overall rubric mean but differ by 31 percentage points on clarity-specific scoring, while data-retrieval and agent-sequence dimensions are unchanged (Li et al., 2026c). Multi-turn artifact reuse. Plan-Execute and Supervisor-Specialist architectures score similarly on single-turn Pass1 but differ by 4.2 on turn-2-to-5 latency due to cross-turn artifact reuse; dimension invisible in single-turn benchmarks (Li et al., 2026b). Retrieval-strategy trade-off. Single-pass RAG and agentic multi-hop retrieval show 50 68% vs. 90% accuracy with 4.510 token inflation (Li et al., 2026a). Neither dominates; the right choice depends on deployment constraints aggregate scores do not surface.

sections.title: 2.2 LLM-as-Judge Measurement is Reflexive
sections.content: Most leaderboards depend on LLM-as-judge scoring (Zheng et al., 2023), which is itself measurement instrument with model-specific biases. As judge models evolve, ranking shifts; as judge prompts are adjusted, scores move. The leaderboard risks measuring its own judge as much as the systems it evaluates. Two independent efforts demonstrate that judgeindependent measurement is feasible. Condition Insight (ODonncha et al., 2026) introduces the Condition Agreement Rate (CAR) by comparing LLMassigned classifications to parallel rule-based pipeline; reported CAR moves from 0.68 to 0.91 under constrained-prompting design, 20pp improvement attributable to prompting rather than to backbone-model choice. ARE/Gaia2 (Froger et al., 2025) checks executed trajectories against humanannotated oracle DAGs with hard, causality, and timing checks that require no LLM evaluation; the reported verifier achieves 0.99 precision and 0.95 recall against 450 hand-labeled trajectories. third data point sharpens the concern: in the PHMForge 3 benchmark (Feng et al., 2026), LLM-as-judge interrater reliability on 30-scenario stratified sample reaches only Krippendorff α = 0.61, well below the humanhuman range α [0.74, 0.82] on the same sample. The judge is itself weaker rater than the experts it is meant to surrogate; finding that generalizes the methodological point: leaderboard with no judge-independent component has no anchor against which to detect judge drift.

sections.title: Deployment Question
sections.content: Deployed systems do not encounter the training set or the leaderboard set. They encounter scenarios that are either (i) distributionally similar to held-out cases, (ii) distributionally distinct (cross-domain transfer), or (iii) adversarially perturbed by user phrasing. In-sample mean predicts none of these directly. Exgentics analysis observed cross-benchmark rank correlations of 0.320.85 across six heterogeneous benchmarks (Bandel et al., 2026), concluding that current architectures do not achieve robust generalization but instead optimize for specific task distributions. We extend this concern: even within single domain, leaderboards should test rank stability under distributional shifts. Anchoring this in earlier NLP work: Ethayarajh and Jurafsky (2020) note that leaderboard ranks reflect users utility function only by accident; Dehghani et al. (2021) document the benchmark lottery in which subsample choice reorders rankings; Recht et al. (2019) provide the classical ImageNet result that ranks fall under modest distributional shift. recent agent-side data point sharpens the concern: Patel et al. (2026) analyze the CODS-2025 AssetOpsBench challenge (149 teams). Publichidden Spearman is ρ = 0.69 for planning (n = 20, robustly positive) and ρ = 0.13 for execution (n = 13, = 0.71, statistically indistinguishable from zero). With = 13, 95% bootstrap CI on the execution correlation spans roughly [0.64, +0.45]; wide enough that we treat the execution result as suggestive only. The planning track is where the evidence is robust: rankings do correlate, but mean private scores still fall 11.3 points below public, and public scores saturate (8 unique values across 20 teams). The deployment-relevant question is not what is the mean, but does this ranking transfer.

sections.title: 3 The Synthesis
sections.content: The OOD-rank-instability argument above asks whether ranks transfer. The next question is what to measure so that they can. The twelve tiers in Figure 2 are not invented but consolidated from the distinctive contributions of seven source benchmarks plus fourteen parallel implementation studies. Each tier corresponds to measurement dimension that at least one prior work surfaces and that we argue is non-redundant with the others; the roughly twelve count and the orthogonality claim are working hypotheses, tested empirically in the research agenda not in the present paper. The seven core tiers (T1T7) consolidate metrics from prior benchmarks: pass-rate floors (T1), toolcall hygiene (T2), planning-process quality (T3), capability axes (T4), cost-efficiency Pareto (T5), failure-mode taxonomies (T6), and reproducibility (T7). The five deployment-extension tiers (T8 T12) are surfaced by the fourteen implementation studies of Section 5: deployment infrastructure (T8), multi-turn dialog (T9), reasoning-mode adaptivity (T10), knowledge augmentation (T11), and evidence grounding with judge-independent verification (T12). Appendix gives the metric-level definition for each tier along with the prior work that surfaces it. The twelve-tier apparatus is not proposal for maximally comprehensive leaderboard. It is claim that the measurement space of deployed LLM agents has roughly twelve orthogonal-ornear-orthogonal dimensions, that no current leaderboard reports more than four or five of them, and that the gap between current practice and the underlying measurement space drives the systematic over-trusting of benchmark rankings that motivates this position.

sections.title: Criterion
sections.content: The twelve tiers say what to measure. The next question is how to use those measurements to rank configurations. leaderboards purpose is to inform deployment decisions, and deployment decisions depend on out-of-sample performance, not in-sample performance. The right ranking criterion is therefore predictive validity: the correlation between in-sample rank and out-of-sample rank, not in-sample mean. 4 Core Capability Tiers (T1T7) Deployment Extension Tiers (T8T12) T2:ToolHygiene T3:PlanningQual. T4:CapabilityAxes T5:Cost& T1:Success Efficiency T6:FailureModes T7:Integrity T9:Multi-TurnDialog T10:ReasoningMode T12:EvidenceGrounding T11:KnowledgeAug. T8:Deploy.Infra SWE-Bench τ -Bench TaskBench MCP-Bench MCP-Universe ARE AssetOpsBench Exgentic Condition Insight 14 ext. studies (G3G30) Coverage: first-class metric partial or implicit not reported Figure 2: Benchmark tier coverage. No single prior benchmark reports more than four or five tiers; the deployment-extension tiers (T8T12) are absent from almost all of them.

sections.title: 4.2 The Predictive-Validity Score
sections.content: Criterion A: Held-Out Scenarios (mild shift). Stratified random split of an existing benchmark, preserving the joint distribution of subset and category across the split. Tests whether the leaderboard ranking on sample predicts ranking on the full population. Weakest test; passes are uninformative, failures are damning. Criterion B: Cross-Subset Transfer (moderate shift). Rank on 1 subsets, test on the held-out subset; rotate across all subsets. For AssetOpsBenchs six subsets (HVAC chillers, compressor, hydraulic pump, FMSA, PHM, rule logic), this produces 6 6 rank-stability matrix. The most realistic test: you ranked agents on chillers; will the ranking transfer to hydraulic pumps? Perturbation Criterion C: Adversarial (strongest shift). Semantically-equivalent paraphrases of base scenarios across four classes: paraphrase (rewrite query preserving intent), identifier renaming (e.g., Chiller 6 Unit-CHX06), time-window shifting (last week two weeks ago to one week ago), and distractor injection (append irrelevant operational context). configuration that genuinely solved the underlying task should perform equivalently across base and perturbed versions. composite ranking score that combines mean performance with out-of-sample reliability: PV(c) = α Yc β σYc,OOD γ IQR(Yc) where Yc is in-sample mean, σYc,OOD is crossOOD-criterion standard deviation of rank position, and IQR(Yc) is interquartile range of per-scenario scores. The weights α, β, γ are fit on CriterionA holdouts to maximize Spearman correlation between PV rank and Criterion-B/C ranks. We do not finalize the weights or the functional form in this position paper. The score is proposal; the empirical study to fit and validate it is part of the research agenda in Section 6.

sections.title: 4.3 Falsification of the Position
sections.content: For our position to be considered well-supported under empirical validation, the following conditions should hold: Spearman rank correlation between in-sample and OOD rankings ρ < 0.85 across at least two of three OOD criteria (else generalization is high and our concern is moot). Top-3 in-sample configurations leave top5 on out-of-sample in 10% of holdout splits (else top-3 deployment recommendations would not change). 5 Mean-vs.-OOD-variance correlation ρPearson > 0.2 (else high-performing configurations are not disproportionately unstable). PV-ranking top-10 differs from mean-ranking top-10 with Jaccard overlap < 0.85 (else the proposed methodology offers no different deployment guidance). We commit to publishing the position as refuted if these conditions fail under controlled experiment. The first condition is already partially supported: Patel et al. (2026) report execution-track public private Spearman ρ = 0.13, well below the 0.85 threshold, on the same benchmark family this paper studies. The remaining conditions require the controlled study outlined in Section 6. Figure 3 shows the headline improvement each team reports against its own baseline. Bars are colored by primary extension axis to match Figure 1. The horizontal axis is logarithmic because the range spans roughly 1.5 to 3500. Annotations state what was held constant or co-improved (quality preserved, accuracy gain, etc.). Three groups (G9, G19, G30) are not plotted because their headline findings are not magnitudes (F1 ceiling, workflow-dependent backbone swap, substrate ablation); see Appendix for those.

sections.title: 5 Convergent Architectural Sensitivity
sections.content: Where Section 4 prescribes ranking criterion, this section asks whether the underlying measurements are even being collected. We report findings from fourteen implementation studies of MCP-based industrial agents, grouped by the extension axis each touches most directly (matching Figure 1). Figure 3 previews the magnitude of improvement each team reports against its own baseline; the cases below explain what each bar represents and which evaluation dimension it implicates. The studies share institutional context as noted in Section 1, so we treat their convergence as architectural sensitivity rather than independent replication. Variances and confidence intervals are reported where the source studies provide them.

sections.title: 5.1 Reasoning mode: cost of extended
sections.content: thinking plan-execute profiling study toggled extendedthinking on Gemma-4-26B planner over 40 multiagent AssetOpsBench scenarios served through vLLM on an A100 (Li et al., 2026c). Reasoning-on raised total latency by 21.5% (15.08 to 18.32 s) and planning latency by 41.9%. The quality gain was non-uniform: clarity improved 31 percentage points (61% to 92%) and hallucination dropped 7 pp (12% to 5%), but data retrieval and agentsequence correctness were unchanged. The overall rubric mean hides where reasoning helps and where it does not; per-rubric reasoning sensitivity belongs in any leaderboard that reports reasoning-mode results.

sections.title: Knowledge Plugin
sections.content: A skills-and-knowledge-plugin study compared single-pass RAG against an agentic multi-hop Knowledge-Plugin MCP server on ten AssetOpsBench scenarios (Li et al., 2026a). With Llama-4Maverick-17B, RAG reached 5068% rubric accuracy at 8.920 end-to-end, while the Knowledge Plugin reached 90% at 114146 with 4.5 10 token inflation. cross-model split sharpened the trade-off: Granite-3-8B reached 60% at 91 on the same Knowledge Plugin pipeline, weaker model with faster path and lower ceiling. Neither configuration dominates, and leaderboard reporting only accuracy hides the deployment regime that selects between them. Retrieval strategy (single-pass, multi-hop, hybrid) should therefore be declared submission property.

sections.title: 5.3 Evaluation methodology:
sections.content: judge-independent governance Two deployed systems and one new benchmark all show that LLM-as-judge scoring needs an external anchor. Condition Insight, an industrial CMMS reasoning framework, verifies LLMassigned condition classifications against parallel rule-based pipeline and reports Condition Agreement Rate (CAR) climbing from 0.68 to 0.91 under constrained-prompting design, promptlevel gain rather than backbone-model gain (ODonncha et al., 2026). ARE/Gaia2s DAG oracle does analogous trajectory-level verification, achieving 0.99 precision and 0.95 recall against 450 hand-labeled trajectories (Froger et al., 2025). PHMForge sharpens the concern from the judge side: LLM-as-judge inter-rater reliability sits at Krippendorff α = 0.61 on 30-scenario stratified sample, well below the human-human range α [0.74, 0.82] on the same data (Feng et al., 2026). leaderboard with no judge-independent 6 G7 disk cache 3500 (predict-only) quality preserved (8 G16 Hedged FMSR 36 quality 0.6600.675 (INT4 3B vs 70B) G8 Scenario-Gen 8 quality preserved (74.2 vs 73.8) G20 QLoRA 5.7 token reduction AT-F1 +0.18 (0.470.65) G7 MCP-batched fleet 6.06 quality preserved G5 Multi-Turn 4.2 (turn 2-5 vs 1) completion +30pp G9 Temporal cache 3.48 end-to-end F1 cap 0.64 (cache-validity) G27 torch.compile + GPU 3.3 quality preserved G14 Confidence-gate 2.34 overall correct hallucination 58pp G12 Verified PE + Self-Ask 1.43 judge mean +8.4pp pass G21 Reasoning-on 1.26 completion clarity +31pp G3 Knowledge Plugin 2.2 accuracy ratio +22pp G23 Visual Inspect AWQ-domain 1.99 +34pp judge pass (0.480.82) Axis colors: Asset Orch. Know. Infra Reas. Eval 1 3 10 30 100 300 1000 3500 Speedup or improvement ratio (log scale) Figure 3: Headline improvement per team vs. own baseline, log-scale, colored by extension axis. G9, G19, G30 omitted (non-magnitude findings; see Table 1). component has no anchor against which to detect judge drift. ning when to call them.

sections.title: 5.4 Evaluation methodology: substrate
sections.content: underspecification The PHMForge benchmark stages 99 SMEauthored prognostics scenarios across eight industrial asset classes, served through 39 algorithmgrounded MCP tools (Feng et al., 2026). It is the only study in our corpus that replaces, rather than extends, the plan-execute base, using ReAct and Claude Code instead. The strongest configuration reached 80.8% pass@1, but three controlled ablations mattered more than the headline number. Replacing MCP tool execution with text-RAG over telemetry-equivalent evidence collapsed remaininguseful-life pass-all-3 from 100% to 20% (5/5 to 1/5) on the lithium-ion class. Cross-equipment transfer dropped pass rate from 84.1% on bearings to 42.7% on motors, 41-point gap on the same scenario format. Operator-style fuzzy queries fell from 80.6% to 48.6% (McNemar = 0.002), and withholding domain tools took completion from 80.8% down to 25%. Substrate, asset, and queryformulation are three deployment-relevant axes invisible to any fixed-substrate, fixed-asset, fixedphrasing leaderboard; orchestration errors dominate failures, and frontier LLMs in the authors words are stronger at calling tools than at plan-

sections.title: 5.5 The Triangulation Claim
sections.content: No one of these findings alone justifies twelvetier apparatus. Taken together, fourteen parallel implementation teams plus seven prior benchmark papers converged on the same set of measurement dimensions, each from distinct architectural starting point. This convergence is the strongest evidence we offer for the synthesis; not new experiments, but convergent architectural sensitivity across many. Table 4 summarizes the mapping from each implementation study to the four high-level evaluation dimensions the EMNLP theme foregrounds; robustness, trustworthiness, generalization, and longitudinal/drift behavior. All four dimensions receive convergent evidence from multiple independent teams. Additional cases. Six further cases (multi-turn artifact reuse, weight-internalized tool knowledge, confidence-gated routing, asset-class extensions to batteries and transformers, caching trustworthiness, and multi-modal visual-inspection extension) are presented in Appendix A; each follows the same evidence-and-lesson structure as the cases above. 7 Rob. Trust. Gen. Long. Profiling (G21) Multi-Turn (G5) 4.2 Skills+KP (G3) Conf. Gate (G14) Temp. Cache (G9) 3.5 31pp 69 +22 0.64 QLoRA (G20) 83% 19 Transformer (G8) 74. 8 Battery (G7) PHMForge (G30) SmartGrid (G12) FMSR Disp. (G16) TSFM Back. (G19) 6 41 2.4k 36 0.61 + Li-ion 8 cls. Wind 13 TSFM Opt. (G27) 3.3 Figure 4: Evidence heatmap: fourteen extension studies (rows) four evaluation dimensions (cols)

sections.title: Implications for Leaderboard Design
sections.content: The position translates into three concrete proposals for how leaderboards should be structured. Proposal 1: Declared configuration columns. Beyond Model and Pass1, submissions should declare: Architecture (Plan-Execute, SupervisorSpecialist, Solo, etc.), Reasoning Mode (off, on, adaptive), Retrieval Strategy (none, single-pass, multi-hop, internalized), Prompt-Constraint Level (none, light, constrained), and Verifier Type (none, conclusion-only, trajectory-only, both). Each is non-empty axis whose value affects how performance should be attributed; conflating across them produces misleading rankings. The SmartGridBench study (Bhandari et al., 2026) provides direct empirical motivation for this proposal: in 2,420-trajectory experiment over the plan-execute base agent, varying transport (direct vs. MCP) and orchestration (Plan-Execute vs. Verified PE vs. Self-Ask) independently shows that MCP standardization adds latency without quality gain, while orchestration changes alone raise pass rate from 43.2% to 55.5%. leaderboard collapsing across these two axes attributes orchestration wins to transport, or vice versa. Proposal 2: Layered presentation. leaderboard reader should see (Layer 1) small headline table with PV rank, (Layer 2) cost-Pareto plot showing where each configuration sits, (Layer 3) drill-down panels per tier, and (Layer 4) significance and confidence intervals. Each layer answers different question. Headline tables should not aggregate beyond their resolution; details should not bury the headline. Proposal 3: Required submission elements. Multi-run variance, hardware disclosure, declared tier coverage, raw trajectories. The field also needs two community artifacts: shared rule pipeline for judge-independent verification, and an adversarialperturbation suite for Criterion C. (i) Aggregate scores are sufAlternative views. ficient for the model-comparison purpose leaderboards actually serve. On per-model basis, an aggregate score is useful summary; our claim is narrower, that rankings derived from such scores do not transfer. (ii) Predictive validity is itself measured by single correlation and so reproduces the problem. It does not: predictive validity is reported across three OOD criteria with explicit thresholds and bootstrap CIs (Section 4.3), so single number cannot hide the variance behind it. (iii) HELM and Dynabench already address multi-dimensionality. They address it for single-shot models; agents introduce trajectoryand orchestration-level axes that those frameworks do not score (Figure 2).

sections.title: 7 Summary and Outlook
sections.content: This paper consolidated the largest coordinated extension of one agent benchmark we are aware of: fourteen parallel implementation studies, roughly six thousand judged trajectories, six extension axes (asset class, orchestration, knowledge/retrieval, infrastructure, reasoning mode, evaluation methodology) and twelve evaluation tiers. The consolidated picture is that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify the dimensions on which deployed LLM agents are judged; the corrective is to rank by predictive validity rather than in-sample mean, and to report on multi-tier apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions current single-shot multi-metric frameworks (HELM, Dynabench, behavioral testing) do not score. The position is concrete enough to be falsified through three OOD criteria with explicit thresholds. Drawing the fourteen studies forward-looking suggestions together (Appendix F), four field-level recommendations emerge. 1) Declare configurations, not just models. Submission fields for architecture, reasoning mode, retrieval strategy, 8 prompt-constraint level, and verifier type let readers attribute performance to its cause. 2) Rank by transfer, not by mean. Report predictive-validity scores across at least one OOD criterion; treat the in-sample mean as one column among many. 3) Require judge-independent anchor. Every leaderboard should have at least one trajectory-level deterministic verifier (rule pipeline, DAG oracle, or similar) so judge drift is detectable. 4) Adopt persistent, non-stdio infrastructure for the benchmark itself. Three of fourteen studies independently identified MCP-stdio overhead as dominant latency floor; serious deployment-oriented benchmark cannot leave protocol overhead conflated with reasoning ability. These four moves do not require new benchmark; they require different relationship between leaderboards and the deployments they advise.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: We are explicit about what this paper does not establish. Empirical validation is future work. The predictive-validity claim has not been tested at scale by us. We have specified the experiment but have not run it. The position is supported by convergent architectural-sensitivity evidence across fourteen implementation studies, not by controlled randomized trial. Reviewers should evaluate the paper as synthesis and position, not as an empirical finding. Domain specificity. All evidence is drawn from industrial asset operations and maintenance, specifically AssetOpsBench and its extensions. Whether the twelve-tier apparatus generalizes to other MCPbased domains (scientific assistants, customer service, code generation) is open. We frame the apparatus as hypothesis tested within one domain. Tier independence is asserted, not tested. We claim the twelve tiers are roughly orthogonal. We have not empirically tested this; doing so requires the experimental work the position calls for. Reviewers should read tier-orthogonality as working hypothesis. Industrial deployment validity gap. All proposed predictive-validity criteria remain internal to AssetOpsBenchs measurement apparatus. We do not yet have data linking framework rankings to real deployed-system outcomes (operator override rate, incident reduction, false-alarm rate). Closing this gap requires industry partnerships beyond what this paper resolves. Implementation-study epistemic status. The fourteen implementation studies are unpublished implementation reports, not peer-reviewed publications. Their value here is convergence under architectural diversity, not the independent peer review each would warrant as standalone empirical contribution.

sections.title: Ethical Considerations
sections.content: This paper makes no use of human-subjects data, personally-identifiable information, or sensitive deployed-system traces. The implementation studies we synthesize use the publicly released AssetOpsBench dataset (Apache-2.0 license) and synthetic or publicly-available industrial data sources (e.g., NASA PCOE Li-ion cycling data). The position the paper advances; that leaderboards should measure predictive validity; has potential downside: such measurements are more expensive than aggregate scoring and could concentrate evaluation capacity in well-resourced institutions. We flag this as field-organization concern and recommend that reference adversarial-perturbation suites and reference rule pipelines be maintained as community artifacts to mitigate the gap.

sections.title: Cases
sections.content: The following five cases complement the four in Section 5. Each follows the same evidence-andlesson structure as the body cases. Orchestration: multi-turn artifact reuse. multi-turn study replaced AssetOpsBenchs singleagent plan-execute baseline with SupervisorSpecialist (SS) architecture that reuses tool outputs across turns, plus an SSA variant that adds paralleltool execution (Li et al., 2026b). SS dropped tooltime share of wall-clock from 47.3% to 26.3%, planning effectiveness rose from 0.559 to 0.791, schema-failure rate fell 68.7%, and turns 25 ran 4.2 faster than turn 1 (34.3 vs. 145.4 mean). The parallel-tool variant, however, inflated token consumption (3.62M vs. 3.32M for sequential SS, both above the 2.55M plan-execute baseline) and worsened tail latency. Cross-turn artifact-reuse rate is deployment-cost metric invisible to single-turn benchmarks, and the SSA counter-finding shows that win on one axis can be loss on another. Reasoning mode: weight-internalized tool knowledge. QLoRA fine-tuning study internalized AssetOpsBenchs tool catalogs into the adapter weights of Gemma-4-E4B model, eliminating the 2,400-token schema that the informed baseline prepends to every prompt (Agarwal et al., 2026). The fine-tuned model reached AT-F1 0.65 versus the baselines 0.47 and judge score 3.88 versus 2.88, with an 82.6% reduction in input tokens. catastrophic-forgetting probe, however, exposed longitudinal cost: Gemma retained 79.8% of pretraining MCQ accuracy, while Qwen3-4B under the same protocol retained only 61.3%. The implication generalizes beyond this study: any weightmodifying optimization can shift behavior on tasks the benchmark does not test, so retention submetric belongs in the failure-mode panel whenever submitted configuration involves fine-tuning. Reasoning mode: confidence-gated routing. skill-knowledge-augmented agent study audited baseline AssetOpsBench trajectories and found that 35.5% contained unnecessary calls to the expensive Deep-TSFM tool, 96% of them on wrongdomain scenarios (Mazeeva et al., 2026). The proposed fix routes around Deep-TSFM when an FMSR confidence threshold θ is met; sweep over θ {0.5, . . . , 0.95} identified θ = 0.8 as the operating point. At that threshold, overall-correct rose from 13.0% to 30.4%, hallucination fell from 93.5% to 35.6%, and agent-sequence correctness rose from 6.5% to 88.9%. The same intervention shows up as tool-hygiene improvement, an adaptive-routing property, and hallucination reduction at once; the cross-cutting pattern is evidence that the measurement dimensions interact whenever configuration is optimized for deployment. Asset class: batteries and transformers. Two extensions tested whether the AssetOpsBench framework actually generalizes beyond its HVAC origin. One added Smart-Grid Transformer asset with four IEC-standards-grounded tools and an automated scenario-generator (Kumar et al., 2026); the other added Battery-analytics MCP server with eleven tools seeded by NASA PCoE cycling data (Gowda et al., 2026). Both reported 6 to 8 end-to-end speedups under profiling-driven optimization, with quality preserved in the transformer case (composite score 74.2 1.9 versus baseline 73.83.0). The battery study passed 8 of 11 scenarios at 7.4 s, against 44.6 on the 10-cell fleet query baseline. Does the framework extend? is deployment question that no in-domain HVAC leaderboard can answer; cross-domain transfer must be reportable axis. Knowledge augmentation: caching trustworthiness. temporal-semantic caching study layered parameter-aware cache over the AssetOpsBench plan-execute pipeline, with Volatile / Static / Relative / Anchored temporal classifier and DAGlayered parallel execution (Merchant et al., 2026). The combined system achieved 3.48 end-to-end speedup over 80 paraphrase-tier queries, with median 30.6 on cache hits. The caches hit-decision F1 ceiling was 0.64 (recall 0.5625, precision 0.75), limited by parameter-collision false positives in industrial queries where two syntactically similar requests target different physical assets. Caching helps and caching is safe are different claims that require different metrics; cache-induced hallucination must be tracked, not assumed away. Asset class (multi-modal): visual inspection extension. visual inspection extension added Vision-Language MCP server to AssetOpsBench, covering 22 scenarios across four asset classes (pumps, power transformers, induction motors, wind-turbine blades) drawn from public image datasets that AssetOpsBenchs origi12 nal text+timeseries benchmark could not address (Sheikh et al., 2026). The study sweeps ten serving variants on single L4 GPU for Qwen2.5-VL7B and Llama-3-LLaVA-NeXT-8B, isolating the quantization-to-serving stack. Three findings cut against common practice. First, AWQ W4A16 with domain-specific calibration was the only variant that improved both speed and judge accuracy on the primary model (Qwen pass rate 0.48 0.82 with 1.99 latency reduction; the same configuration with generic ultrachat calibration plateaued at 0.73). Second, an aggressive vLLM serving bundle combining FP8 KV-cache, chunked prefill, and prefix caching collapsed Qwens output entirely (0/44, response collapse) while passing on Llama; FP8 KV-cache corruption of long visual-token prefixes is the suspected cause. Third, downscaling input images to 512 pixels produced latency regressions on both model families, indicating the resolution knee for fixed-tile vision encoders sits below 512 px. The broader lesson generalizes: in multi-modal agentic deployments, quantization and serving choices interact with modality-specific encoder behavior in ways that standard NLP benchmarks cannot detect.

sections.title: B Tier Definitions
sections.content: Each tier below names measurement dimension, the source benchmark(s) that surface it as firstclass metric, and representative metric. T1 Success. Headline pass/fail: AssetOpsBenchs Pass1 and Passk, ARE/Gaia2s DAGPass, the six rubric dimensions y1y6. The minimum reportable floor, uninformative beyond it. T2 Tool-Call Hygiene. MCP-Benchs toolname validity, schema compliance, execution success, dependency-order correctness. Nonredundant because hygiene failures occur on otherwise-successful trajectories. T3 Planning Quality. MCP-Benchs five LLM-judged planning axes plus TaskBenchs ROUGE on decomposition, Node/Edge F1, chain-order NED (Shen et al., 2024). Two configurations can succeed by different planning processes. T4 Capability Axes. ARE/Gaia2s seven axes (execution, search, adaptability, time, ambiguity, agent-to-agent, noise) plus TaskBenchs Node/Chain/DAG stratification. Capability profile differs across similarly-ranked models. T5 Cost & Efficiency. MCP-Universe and Gaia2s $/scenario, step count, latency, budget-scaling curves. Cost-quality Pareto position determines deployment viability. T6 Failure Modes. AssetOpsBenchs 14 MAST failure modes augmented with emergent clusters, distractor robustness, recovery rate. Failure modes inform mitigation design beyond pass-rate signal. T7 Integrity & Reproducibility. Multirun variance, prompt-shuffle averaging (MCPBench), validation/test split, judgehuman inter-rater agreement. T8 Deployment Infrastructure. Latency decomposition, MCP-stdio overhead, subprocess-spawn count, optimization-impact ablation, cross-domain transfer. Surfaced by the battery-domain extension (Gowda et al., 2026). T9 Multi-Turn Dialog. Cross-turn artifact reuse, per-turn cost dynamics, context-bloat trade-offs, multi-turn reliability, dialog-level Pass1. Surfaced by (Li et al., 2026b). T10 Reasoning Mode. Per-phase reasoning cost attribution, per-rubric-dimension reasoning sensitivity, adaptive routing precision/recall, reasoning-quality-adjusted latency. Surfaced by (Li et al., 2026c). T11 Knowledge Augmentation. Retrieval recall, multi-hop depth, embedding-index quality, skill-marketplace coverage and selection accuracy. Surfaced by (Li et al., 2026a). T12 Evidence Grounding & Verification. Judge-independent governance (Condition Agreement Rate, ARE hard/causality/timing violations), Unsupported Claim Rate, reasoning stability, verifier-type declarations. The most novel tier, drawn from systems that move beyond LLM-judge-only evaluation (ODonncha et al., 2026; Froger et al., 2025).

sections.title: C Empirical Evidence of Rank Instability
sections.content: Figure 5 aggregates the published rank-correlation evidence cited above. We plot each correlation as point estimate with 95% confidence interval (Fisher z-transform) and vertical reference 13 line at ρ = 0.85; the falsification threshold from Section 4.3. Three observations follow. First, the execution-track correlation from Patel et al. (2026) is statistically indistinguishable from zero: with = 13, the 95% CI spans roughly [0.64, +0.45], wide enough that the point estimate ρ = 0.13 is informative only as not robustly positive. Second, the planning-track correlation is robustly positive (ρ = 0.69, = 20, CI [0.35, 0.87]), but its upper bound still falls at the falsification threshold. Third, Exgentics reported cross-benchmark range (Bandel et al., 2026) of 0.320.85 across six heterogeneous benchmarks straddles the threshold from below. Taken together, the available empirical evidence is consistent with the position; that publicto-hidden rank stability is not reliably above 0.85; without being strong enough to confirm it. The empirical study in Section 6 is designed to close this gap. Per-Study Extended Details The fourteen implementation studies are documented in two stacked tables. Table 1 reports the baseline configuration each study compared against and the magnitude of improvement obtained. Table 2 reports the conclusion drawn from each study and its single most prominent forward-looking suggestion. Group identifiers (G3G30) follow the labels in Figure 1.

sections.title: E Convergence Patterns
sections.content: Independent teams, working from the same base benchmark with different architectures, converged on the same measurement gaps. We list five convergence patterns we identified across the fourteen studies. Each pattern is referenced by name in the main body of the paper. The patterns are not mutually exclusive; many groups fall in more than one. Pattern A: Plan-execute bottleneck localization. Groups G5, G14, G16, and G27 independently localize the same bottleneck to FMSR or TSFM. G5 measures TSFM tool-time as the dominant share of wall-clock (47.3% to 26.3% with supervisor routing). G14 audits the baseline and finds 35.5% of trajectories make redundant TSFM calls (96% wrongdomain). G16 measures FMSR as 559s reduced to 15.5s under hedged dispatch. G27 isolates ttm_forward as 93% of single-call latency. Four teams localize one bottleneck. The base architecture is under-designed, not near-optimal. Pattern B: Prompt-versus-weight tool knowledge. Groups G3, G14, G20, and G21 all improve accuracy by adding more thinking along distinct axes. G3 adds retrieval (KP). G14 adds confidence-gated routing. G20 internalizes the tool catalog into adapter weights, eliminating 82.6% of input tokens. G21 turns on extended-thinking mode. These four are tiling the same trade-off frontier from different sides. Prompt-time versus weight-time tool knowledge is tunable axis, not fixed design choice. Pattern C: MCP transport overhead. Groups G7, G9, G12, and G27 independently measure MCP-stdio subprocess overhead as dominant percall latency floor. G7 reports 56s residual per call. G9 measures discovery as 23s per query, eliminated by disk cache. G12 finds direct inprocess tools (AT-I) outperform MCP (AT-M) on both pass and judge mean. G27 measures modelloading 233ms 26ms via pre-loading. The base benchmark conflates protocol overhead with reasoning ability. Pattern D: Caching trustworthiness. Groups G3, G9, G16, and G27 each add cache. G9 alone surfaces the parameter-collision F1 ceiling at 0.64 as structural failure mode. Caching helps and caching is safe are different claims and require different metrics. Pattern E: Scenario authoring as binding constraint. Groups G7, G8, G12, and G30 all confront the same problem: the original 141-scenario corpus is small. G7 settles for 15 hand-authored battery scenarios. G12 freezes 36-scenario papergrade set. G8 automates scenario generation. G30 scales SME-authored scenarios to 99 across eight asset classes. Scenario-corpus size is the binding constraint on extension work in the field. Forward-Looking Suggestion Clusters The fourteen forward-looking suggestions cluster into four families with strong cross-group convergence. Each cluster aggregates suggestions across multiple studies into single field-level recommendation. Cluster I: Adaptive and learned routing. Five groups (G3, G9, G14, G16, G21) converge on the same idea. Some queries need the expensive path; most do not; static pipeline cannot tell them apart. G3 proposes learned RAG-versus-KP classifier. 14 1 0. 0 0.5 1 CODS planning, n=20 CODS execution, n=13 Exgentic cross-benchmark range Proposed pilot (n=80, target) ρ=0.69 ρ=0.13 6 bench. planned h h n a fi a 5 8 . 0 = ρ Figure 5: Public-to-hidden rank correlations with 95% Fisher-z CIs. Dashed red line is the ρ = 0.85 falsification threshold (Section 4.3). PublicHidden Spearman rank correlation G9 calls for parameter-aware caching. G14 calls for learned confidence calibrator. G16 proposes adaptive ceiling-start dispatch. G21 proposes DistilBERT reasoning-mode router. The aggregate signal: meta-router should be first-class abstraction in the framework. Cluster II: Persistent, non-stdio MCP servers. Three groups (G7, G9, G27) identify subprocess spawn and stdio transport as the residual latency floor and call for persistent server. G7 proposes persistent MCP server modes. G9 proposes cache persistence via FAISS. G27 proposes persistent MCP server through socket reuse. Cluster III: Cross-model and cross-asset generalization. Four groups (G8, G14, G16, G19) call for testing on more than one model or more than one asset class. G8 proposes GPT-4, Llama-4, Mistral Large coverage. G14 proposes pumps, bearings, motors, AHUs. G16 proposes asset domains beyond chillers. G19 proposes an interchangeable model-interface for additional TSFM backbones. Cluster IV: Scenario-corpus expansion. Four groups (G8, G12, G20, G30) call for moving past the 141-scenario corpus. G8 provides the automated tooling. G12 has 25 unjudged scenarios pending. G20 proposes continual-learning protocols for new tools. G30 has public livingbenchmark expansion plan. The aggregate of these four clusters is exactly the position-paper thesis. AssetOpsBench is underspecified along the same four axes that all fourteen groups improvise around: adaptive routing, persistent infrastructure, cross-model/asset coverage, and scenario authoring. Predictive-Validity Score: Methodological Notes The predictive-validity score PV(c) = α Yc βσYc,OOD γ IQR(Yc) is proposal, not finalized scoring rule. This appendix records methodological notes for the empirical study we propose in Section 6. Weight fitting. The weights α, β, γ are fit on Criterion-A holdouts to maximize Spearman correlation between predictive-validity rank and Criterion-B/C ranks. We propose constrained optimization over the simplex α + β + γ = 1 with all 0, and recommend reporting the full Pareto frontier rather than single point estimate. Statistical power. For 80 paired ranks, Spearman ρ = 0.85 versus ρ = 0.95 is distinguishable at α = 0.01 with power above 0.9 (Fisher-z transform). For 1,200 perturbed observations (120 scenarios top-10 configurations), mean-metric differences of 0.05 are detectable with power above 0.95. The proposed pilot is under-powered for fine subgroup analyses and we will report subgroup results as exploratory only. Pre-registered pilot decision rule. If pilot train holdout Spearman exceeds 0.95 across all three architectures, we revise the central claim downward: AssetOpsBench rankings appear highly stable at i.i.d. holdout and the full study is unlikely to clear the ρ < 0.85 falsification threshold. The paper pivots to methodology contribution. If pilot correlation falls in (0.65, 0.95) we proceed to the full study as designed. Below 0.65 the claim is so strongly supported that the empirical bar can be cleared with reduced scope. Required submission infrastructure. Two community artifacts are required before any leader15 Group Axis Baseline Magnitude vs. baseline G3 Skills+KP Knowledge/Retrieval Single-pass RAG (top-k=3, Maverick-17B) Llama-490% vs. 5068% acc.; 4.510 tokens; 7 latency G5 Multi-Turn Orchestration Single-agent plan-execute Planning +41.6%; completion +30.4%; tool-time 47.3% 26.3%; turn 1 vs. 25 4.2 G7 Battery Asset Class Plain plan-execute on per-cell MCP, no cache or batching 6.06 end-to-end; disk cache 7.0 0.002 (3500) G8 former TransEvaluation Methodology Sequential scenario generator, no cache, Llama-3.3-70B 8 at =50; quality 74.21.9 vs. 73.83.0 (preserved) G9 Temporal Cache SmartG12 GridBench Knowledge/Retrieval No-cache plan-execute, Qwen3 emb. + reranker 30.6 on cache hits; 3.48 overall; F1 cap 0.64 Evaluation Methodology AT-I direct 8B/vLLM) in-process tools (Llama-3.1V-PE+Self-Ask: 0.620 mean / 55.5% pass vs. 0.474 / 43.2% G14 Gate Conf. Reasoning Mode Static plan-execute on four MCP servers FMSR G16 Dispatch Infrastructure Sequential FMSR w/ cloud-hosted Llama-3.3-70B G19 Backend TSFM Infrastructure Unoptimized TSFM (TTM) w/ HF Trainer Overall 13% 30.4%; hallu. 93.5% 35.6%; seq. 6.5% 88.9% Hedged 36 (559 s15.5 s); INT4 3B: 0.675 vs. 0.660; DB prefetch hurts Wind Turbines (acc. 0.60 0.43) Forecasting 33.3%; Chronos 12.8 faster forecasting but 81 slower on fine-tuning G20 QLoRA Reasoning Mode Unfine-tuned Gemma-4-E4B with full schemas inline AT-F1 +0.18; 61.3% MCQ vs. Gemma 79.8% tokens 82.6%; Qwen3 retains G21 Profiling Reasoning Mode Gemma-4-26B vLLM/A100 reasoning-off via Clarity +31pp; +21.5%; planning +41.9% completion +16pp; latency G27 Opt. sections.title: TSFM
sections.content: Infrastructure Untouched TSFM agent w/ HF Trainer, CPU ttm_forward 69%; model load 89%; bf16 regresses 3.3 2.6 PHMEvaluation Methodology Text-RAG over telemetry-equivalent evidence (Claude Opus 4.6) MCP vs. RAG: 80.6% vs. 48.6% (McNemar p=0.002); RUL: 100%20% G23 Visual Inspect Asset Class (multi-modal) FP16 Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline on 22 visual scenarios (4 asset classes), L4 GPU AWQ W4A16 + domain calibration: pass 0.480.82, latency 9.4 4.8 (1.99); L2 full-bundle FP8KV: 0/44 (response collapse); L3 image_512: latency regression Table 1: Per-study setup: baseline configuration and magnitude of improvement. Axis pill matches Figure 1. board can report predictive-validity. reference shared rule pipeline for judge-independent verification (CAR computation). reference adversarialperturbation suite: 30 base scenarios with one perturbation per class (paraphrase, identifier renaming, time-window shifting, distractor injection). Headline Chart per Study (Source Pointers) For each study, the source reports headline chart that supports the single most striking finding cited in the main body of this paper. These chart references are pointers for readers verifying claims; we do not reproduce the charts here. Each entry cites the source report where the chart appears. G3 Skills+KP (Li et al., 2026a); Token-usage bar chart (Knowledge Plugin vs. RAG, 10 scenarios). G5 Multi-Turn (Li et al., 2026b); Per-turn latency line chart by architecture. tion ablation table (Naive +Parallel +Compile +Batched +Disk-cache). G8 Transformer (Kumar et al., 2026); Pipeline-time vs. scenario-count table (N {10, 25, 50}, baseline vs. optimized). G9 Temporal Cache (Merchant et al., 2026); Per-row baseline-vs-optimized latency scatter. G12 SmartGridBench (Bhandari et al., 2026); Core matrix table (seven cells judge mean and pass rate). G14 Conf. Gate (Mazeeva et al., 2026); Threshold sweep table for the gated configuration (θ {0.5, . . . , 0.95}). G16 FMSR Dispatch (Jebbouri et al., 2026); Quantization model-family accuracy bar chart vs. cloud-hosted Llama-3.3-70B baseline. G19 TSFM Backend (Go et al., 2026); TTM G7 Battery (Gowda et al., 2026); Optimizaforecasting stage-breakdown bar chart. Group Axis Conclusion Forward-looking G3 Skills+KP Knowledge/Retrieval Knowledge plugins act as inference-time scaling: accuracy at cost. Binary classifier to route RAG vs. Knowledge Plugin. G5 Multi-Turn Orchestration Supervisor-specialist + artifact reuse beats singleagent plan-execute. Parallel specialists with evidence summarization. G7 Battery Asset Class MCP-stdio subprocess overhead dominates inference. Persistent (non-stdio) MCP server mode. G8 former TransEvaluation Methodology Standards-grounded (IEC) auto scenario generation scales benchmark authoring. Cross-model judge; human-annotation replacement. G9 Temporal Cache SmartG12 GridBench Knowledge/Retrieval Pure semantic similarity is structurally insufficient for parameter-rich queries. Parameter-aware (entity, sensor, time-window) caching. Evaluation Methodology Protocol, orchestration, and evidence-accounting are separate variables. Extend hosted-70B coverage; judge remaining 25 scenarios. G14 Gate Conf. Reasoning Mode The strongest agent is the one that knows when tool is unnecessary. Learned confidence calibrator; per-asset thresholds. FMSR G16 Dispatch G19 Backend TSFM Infrastructure Infrastructure G20 QLoRA Reasoning Mode FMSR is the dominant bottleneck; crossasset prefetch transfer is non-trivial. Hedged dispatch + INT4 small models for binary relevancy. No universal TSFM backbone; wins on one workflow are losses on another. Standardized TSAD preprocessing; pluggable backends. Tool knowledge moves from prompt to weights, but with retention cost. Continual learning for new tools; rank-aware retention. G21 Profiling Reasoning Mode Reasoning pays its cost in planning, returns it in downstream quality. Adaptive thinking router (DistilBERT, 66M). G27 Opt. G30 Forge

sections.title: TSFM
sections.content: Infrastructure Agentic-ML optimization = model optimization; bf16 hurts MLP-Mixer. Persistent MCP via socket reuse; sensor batching. PHMEvaluation Methodology Algorithm-grounded MCP tools are necessary substrate; orchestration dominates failures. High-performance agent serving; community scenario expansion. G23 Visual Inspect Asset Class (multi-modal) Domain-calibrated AWQ quantization is the rare optimization that improves both speed and judge accuracy; aggressive serving bundles (FP8-KV) can silently collapse VLM output quality. Isolate FP8-KV from other vLLM flags; ablate image-resolution between 256384 px; extend to chillers and AHUs when public image data appears. Table 2: Per-study interpretation: the conclusion the source report draws and the single forward-looking suggestion it most directly emphasizes. G20 QLoRA (Agarwal et al., 2026); 5dimension 4-configuration grouped bar chart of LLM-judge scores. G21 Profiling (Li et al., 2026c); Per-scenario latency breakdown table (reasoning-off vs. on). G27 TSFM Optim. (Vinod et al., 2026); Ablation incremental-speedup table. G30 PHMForge (Feng et al., 2026); Percategory pass-all-3 table (RAG-fuzzy / RAGexplicit / MCP-fuzzy / MCP-explicit). G23 Visual Inspect (Sheikh et al., 2026); 22scenario sweep table (E2E latency, p50, judge pass) across L0L3 quantization/serving variants for two VL models.
